text: The chalk dust coats your feet white. Each step leaves a powdery mark on the short grass.
A skylark rises from the turf, its song sharp against the empty sky.
The hills roll away in gentle curves. Green turf stretched tight over white bone.
Flint shards catch the light like black teeth. They cut through the chalk in jagged seams.
Your fingers brush the grass tops. The blades spring back, dense and wiry.
The downland smells of crushed herbs and dry earth. Wind carries the scent of sheep.
White soil crumbles between your fingers. It leaves chalky streaks on your palms.
The turf bounces under your weight. Each footfall releases a faint dust cloud.
Larks call from three directions at once. Their voices overlap and fade.
A piece of flint rolls under your boot. The sharp edge draws a thin line of blood.
The grass grows in tight cushions. Centuries of wind and grazing keep it low.
Chalk shows through where the soil wears thin. The white gleams against green stems.
Grey limestone stretches ahead in flat sheets. Grass grows thick between the cracks.
The stream runs clear over white stones. Your boots splash through ankle-deep water.
Ash leaves rustle overhead. Their shadows shift across the pale rock.
You step from stone to stone across the pavement. The gaps between slabs drop into darkness.
Water echoes from somewhere below your feet. The sound comes up through narrow fissures.
The valley curves between limestone walls. Green turf slopes down to the water.
Your palm scrapes against the rough stone edge. Tiny shells press into the rock surface.
A blackbird calls from the ash grove. The notes bounce off the grey cliffs.
The air smells of wet stone and moss. Water seeps from cracks in the pavement.
You duck under low ash branches. Bark flakes away under your grip.
The cave mouth opens black in the cliff face. Cool air flows out over your skin.
Fast water cuts through the green dale. Limestone boulders crowd the stream bed.
Purple heather spreads across the sandstone in thick mats. The rock beneath shows rust-red and amber through the stems.
Your boots scrape against the gritty surface. Sandstone dust clings to the leather.
Silver birch trunks catch the light between dark heather clumps. Their bark peels in papery strips.
The heather releases its dry scent as you brush through. Pollen dusts your legs yellow.
A sandstone overhang juts from the slope above. Its underside stays cool and damp.
Wind rattles through birch leaves with a sound like rain. The heather barely moves.
Warm stone radiates heat against your palm. The surface feels rough as grinding grain.
Sheep paths wind between the heather bushes. The earth shows black where hooves have worn it bare.
Sandstone fragments crunch underfoot. Each step sends small stones skittering downhill.
The overhang's shadow falls across purple flowers. Moss grows thick where water drips.
Birch catkins hang pale against the dark rock face. They sway on threads in the breeze.
Heather honey scent drifts on the warm air. Bees work the tiny bells with steady humming.
Grey granite breaks through thin soil in weathered blocks. Lichen maps yellow and orange across the stone faces.
Wind hums constant through the tussock grass. The sound never stops, never changes pitch.
Your boots sink into black peat between the grass clumps. Water seeps up around your ankles with each step.
A tor rises from the moor like stacked bones. Ravens wheel around its crown, their calls sharp against the granite.
The air smells of wet earth and rotting vegetation. Bog myrtle releases its bitter scent when you brush past.
Stunted oaks huddle in the valley bottom. Their branches twist away from the prevailing wind.
Purple moor grass rustles dry against itself. The sound follows you across the open ground.
Your hands scrape rough granite as you climb the outcrop. Crystals of quartz catch what little sun breaks through.
Sphagnum moss squelches underfoot, bright green and sodden. It holds water like a sponge in your fist.
The wind carries the smell of rain from the western hills. Dark clouds build above the highest tors.
Cotton grass nods white tufts above the bog. They move together like a flock of sheep in the distance.
Stone circles crown the nearest hill. Each granite block stands taller than a man, worn smooth by centuries of weather.
The valley walls rise black on both sides. Water crashes over stone somewhere ahead.
Your feet slip on wet slate. The rock splits in thin sheets under your weight.
Oak branches tangle overhead. Hazel shoots crowd the narrow path between trunks.
The river runs brown with peat. It cuts deep channels through the dark stone.
Moss grows thick on every surface. Your fingers sink into the green cushions.
Ferns brush against your legs. Water drips from their broad leaves onto your feet.
The air smells of rotting wood and wet earth. Something mushroomy rises from the ground.
Slate chips scatter down the slope. Each step sends small stones tumbling into the valley.
Hazel nuts crack under your boots. The broken shells stick to the damp ground.
The river's voice echoes off the valley walls. Stone and water make a steady roar.
Your hands come away black from touching the rock. The slate dust stains your palms.
Oak roots grip the steep ground. They twist between the broken slabs of stone.
Oak trunks rise like pillars, their bark furrowed deep as a man's palm. The canopy blocks the sky.
Your feet sink into black earth with each step. Clay clings to your heels.
Leaves rustle overhead without wind. Something large moves through the branches.
The air smells of wet wood and rotting leaves. Mushrooms sprout from fallen logs.
Elm roots twist across the path, thick as your waist. You step over them carefully.
Light filters green through the canopy. Shadows shift on the forest floor.
A branch cracks somewhere behind you. The sound echoes between the trees.
Lime flowers hang in pale clusters above your head. Their scent is sharp and sweet.
The ground squelches under your weight. Water seeps up around your footprints.
Moss covers the oak bark in thick patches. It feels damp beneath your fingers.
Branches interlace overhead like a roof. The sky disappears in green darkness.
Dead leaves carpet the earth, ankle-deep. They crunch and compress as you walk.
Gravel crunches underfoot. Each stone is smooth, ground round by ice.
Meltwater seeps between your toes. The ground squelches with each step.
Young birch saplings bend in the wind. Their pale bark peels in papery strips.
The air smells of wet stone and iron. Water pools in every hollow.
Gray lichen crusts the larger boulders. It crumbles at your touch, powdering your fingers.
A stream cuts through the debris field. It gurgles over rounded stones, cold and quick.
Your breath clouds in the chill air. Ice glazes the edges of shallow pools.
The wind carries no scent of earth, only rock dust and ice. It cuts through your cloak.
Scattered stones lie heaped in ridges. Some are large as your head, others fine as sand.
Birch leaves rustle, still small and bright green. They whisper against the constant wind.
Water drips from overhanging stones. Each drop rings hollow in the silence.
The ground tilts and dips without pattern. Boulders jut up like broken teeth.
Reed stems crack underfoot. Water seeps through the woven mat, cold against your ankles.
Mallards burst from the shallows in a thunder of wings. Droplets spray your face as they climb.
The mud sucks at your heel with each step. Air bubbles pop and release the smell of rotting vegetation.
Mist drifts between the reed heads. The far shore disappears behind grey walls.
A heron stands motionless in the channel. Its yellow eye tracks your movement through the stems.
Sedge grass cuts your palm as you push through. The blade leaves a thin line of blood.
Water gurgles between the roots. Something large moves beneath the surface, disturbing the silt.
The island rises from the marsh like a green fist. Hawthorn bushes crown its muddy slopes.
Curlews call across the flats. Their voices echo off the water in long, falling notes.
Reed pollen dusts your shoulders yellow. The air tastes thick and green in your throat.
Your foot finds solid ground beneath the water. The gravel bed shifts and settles under your weight.
Coots scream from their hidden nests. The sound cuts through the morning like flint on bone.
The ice wall rises sheer and blue-white, taller than twenty men. Meltwater streams from cracks in its face.
Your boots crunch through gravel and clay. Stones jut at odd angles from the churned earth.
Water rushes over stones with a constant roar. The sound drowns out your footsteps.
A granite boulder sits alone on the muddy slope. It is larger than a hut, smooth-sided.
The air smells of wet stone and raw earth. Your breath steams in white puffs.
Ridges of rubble stretch across the valley floor. Each mound is higher than your head.
Ice groans and cracks somewhere in the white distance. The sound echoes off rock walls.
Your fingers ache within moments of removing your mittens. The cold bites through wool and leather.
Brown water pools in the hollows between stones. Ice forms at the edges overnight.
Mud squelches between your toes through worn boot-soles. The ground gives way with each step.
Wind carries ice-crystals that sting your cheeks. They melt instantly on warm skin.
A line of boulders marks where the ice once stood. Lichens grow orange and yellow on their north faces.
Water drips from the ceiling onto your shoulders. Each drop strikes cold against your skin.
The passage narrows until your elbows scrape against wet limestone.
Your breath mists white in the frigid air. The stone beneath your feet is slick with moisture.
Echoes of your footsteps return from somewhere deep ahead. The sound stretches and fades into silence.
Black water pools in the carved hollows of the floor. Your bare feet splash through the shallow puddles.
The walls weep constant moisture. Mineral deposits coat the stone in pale crusts.
A steady plinking of water marks time in the darkness. The drops fall into unseen pools below.
Cold air moves past your face from deeper passages. It carries the smell of wet earth and old stone.
Your fingers trace grooves worn smooth by centuries of flowing water.
The ceiling drops lower with each step forward. Soon you must duck to continue.
Stone grit crunches underfoot between the wet patches. Your knees bump against jutting rock.
The darkness swallows the sound of your breathing. Only the dripping water proves you still move through space.
The white chalk dust rises with each footfall, hanging in the still air before settling on your legs. Flint shards glitter like broken glass scattered across the pale earth.
Skylarks climb invisible ladders above the rolling hills, their songs sharp and clear in the windless sky. The short grass springs back immediately where you step.
Your shadow falls black and defined on the white turf. Heat shimmers rise from the chalk slopes, blurring the distant ridgelines.
The downland stretches unbroken to the horizon under the cloudless dome. Each flint nodule casts a small shadow on the bright ground.
Grasshoppers tick in the warm turf around your ankles. The chalk reflects the sun's glare, forcing you to squint as you climb the gentle slope.
Rain darkens the chalk to grey. Water pools in the flint-scattered hollows between tufts of grass.
Your boots squelch through sodden turf. The white soil beneath shows through in muddy patches.
Skylarks huddle silent in the wet grass. Their usual song is lost to the steady drumming of rain on earth.
Sharp flint edges glisten black under the downpour. Water runs in thin streams down the curved hillsides.
The springy turf gives way underfoot, releasing dark water with each step. Chalk dust turns to pale mud between your toes.
The chalk turns grey under the fine rain, darker in the wheel ruts that score the hillside.
Water beads on the short grass blades. Your feet sink slightly into the softened turf with each step.
Flint nodules glisten black where the drizzle strikes them. The wet stone shows veins of grey and white.
A skylark's song cuts through the damp air above. Droplets gather on the grass stems, bending them toward the earth.
The rain mists across the down, blurring the far hills to pale shapes. Your woolen cloak grows heavy with moisture.
The chalk vanishes into grey three paces ahead. Your feet find the familiar springy turf, but the hills roll away into nothing.
A skylark calls from somewhere above the fog. The sound hangs in the white air, directionless.
Flint chips glint wetly at your feet before the mist swallows them again. The white soil beneath shows dark with moisture.
The fog presses against your face, cold and damp. You taste chalk dust on your lips as you climb the invisible slope.
Tufts of grass emerge from the grey like small islands. The downland spreads endlessly around you, all of it hidden.
The wind drives across the chalk hills in steady gusts. Your feet sink slightly into the white soil with each step. Short grass bends flat against the slopes.
Skylarks call from somewhere above but the wind tears their songs into fragments. The sound comes and goes as the air shifts direction.
Flint nodules scatter across the hillside, their dark surfaces polished smooth. The wind whistles between them with sharp, hollow notes.
The springy turf cushions your steps but offers no shelter from the constant buffeting. White chalk dust rises from the path and stings your eyes.
Wind-carved furrows run down the steepest slopes where rain has washed the chalk away. The exposed flint creates ridges that catch and funnel the gusts.
Lightning splits the chalk hills into stark white ridges and black hollows. The turf springs back under your bare feet, already darkening with rain.
Thunder rolls across the downs like stones tumbling in a barrow. Flint shards glitter wet in the grass where the storm has washed them clean.
Rain pounds the white soil into pale mud that clings to your ankles. A skylark drops from the grey sky, wings folded against the wind.
The storm turns the short grass into a carpet of bent blades. Water runs in new streams down the chalk, cutting thin white channels toward the valley.
Hail strikes the flint scattered across the downs with sharp clicks. The sound echoes off the wet chalk like bones striking stone.
Snow settles into the chalk, turning the white soil grey-white. The short turf crunches under your feet, ice crystals clinging to each blade.
Flakes drift across the rolling hills, blurring their edges. A skylark's call cuts through the falling snow, then fades.
The flint pieces scattered in the turf catch snowflakes on their dark surfaces. You hear the soft whisper of snow landing on the springy grass.
Snow fills the hollows between the downs, pooling in white drifts. The wind carries flakes horizontally across the exposed slopes.
Your breath steams in the cold air as you climb the chalk ridge. Snow melts on the dark flint, leaving wet patches in the white ground.
The chalk cracks underfoot like broken pottery. White fragments scatter across the frozen turf.
Frost coats each blade of grass in crystal sheaths. The downland glitters under pale sunlight.
Your breath steams as you climb the frozen slope. The turf crunches with each step, hard as stone.
Black flint juts from the white hillside like broken teeth. Ice fills the gaps between the stones.
A skylark calls from somewhere above the ridge. The sound cuts sharp through the cold air.
The chalk dust clings to your feet through the thin haze. White soil stretches ahead, softened by the gauze of air.
Skylarks call from somewhere above the mist. Their voices drift down through the pale light, disembodied.
The turf springs back with each step, damp from the hanging moisture. Flint shards glint dully through the haze.
Rolling hills fade to gray outlines in the distance. The soft light blurs their edges into the white sky.
Your shadow barely marks the chalky ground. The haze swallows the horizon, leaving only the grass beneath your boots.
The limestone stretches flat and grey beneath clear sky. Cracks between the stone slabs hold rainwater that glints in the sunlight.
Ash leaves flutter bright green against the pale rock. The stream cuts through the dale, its water so clear you see every pebble on the bottom.
Sunlight strikes the cave mouth and stops. Beyond the entrance, darkness swallows the limestone passage completely.
The grey pavement radiates heat under the open sky. Your feet find purchase in the deep grooves between stone blocks.
Stream water runs fast over white limestone beds. The sound echoes off the valley walls in the still air.
Water pools in the limestone joints, turning grey cracks into dark lines. Rain drums against the ash leaves overhead.
The stream runs brown with runoff from the valley sides. Its voice rises above the steady patter on stone.
Rain darkens the limestone pavement to charcoal. Your boots slip on the wet rock surface.
Water streams from the cave mouth, carrying the smell of wet stone and earth. The entrance glistens black in the grey light.
Ash trees bend under the weight of water on their leaves. Drops fall steadily from branch to branch, then to the green grass below.
Water beads on the grey limestone slabs. Your feet slip on the wet stone pavements.
The ash trees drip steadily. Each leaf releases its gathered moisture onto the path below.
Steam rises from the valley floor where warm earth meets cool rain. The green grass darkens to black in the hollow.
The stream runs louder than before, fed by runoff from the limestone terraces. Clear water foams white over the rocks.
Drizzle mists the cave mouth. Water tracks down the rock face in thin silver lines.
Grey limestone blocks vanish into white mist three paces ahead. Your boots scrape against invisible cracks in the pavement.
Water rushes somewhere below but you cannot see the stream. The sound echoes off stone walls you cannot locate.
An ash tree materializes from the fog, its trunk black with moisture. Droplets fall from leaves onto the pale rock.
The green valley floor disappears beneath thick white air. Only the limestone beneath your feet confirms solid ground.
Cave mouths gape black in the grey stone face. Fog pours from their openings like slow breath.
Wind tears through the ash leaves with a sound like rushing water. The grey limestone pavement stretches bare and polished between the thrashing branches.
Your boots slip on wet limestone slabs as wind drives spray from the fast stream across the dale. Water beads and runs in the carved channels of the pavement.
The cave mouth exhales cool air that meets the warm wind in visible currents. Ash seeds spiral down into the dark opening.
Wind howls across the limestone terraces and funnels into the green valley below. The clear stream runs white where it breaks over fallen rocks.
Branches crack overhead as the wind bends the ash trees toward the grey pavement. Leaves scatter across the limestone like green coins.
Thunder cracks above the limestone cliffs. Water sheets across the grey pavements, pooling in the deep fissures between stone blocks.
The stream runs brown with mud, twice its normal width. Ash branches whip against the valley walls, leaves stripped and scattered.
Rain hammers the cave mouth, sending spray ten feet into the darkness. The sound echoes deep into the limestone throat.
Lightning illuminates the dale for three seconds. Grey pavements gleam like metal, water rushing between the stone joints.
Wind drives rain horizontal across the valley floor. The ash trees bend nearly double, their white bark streaming wet.
Snow settles in the cracks between limestone blocks, white lines cutting through grey stone. The ash trees stand bare, their black branches catching flakes that melt on contact.
The stream runs dark between snow-dusted banks. Ice forms at the edges where the water slows, thin sheets that crack under falling snow.
Snowflakes disappear into the cave mouth, swallowed by the dark opening in the limestone face. The rock around the entrance gleams wet where snow melts on contact.
Your boots slip on limestone pavement turned slick with melting snow. The grey stone darkens under the wet flakes, each block outlined in white powder.
Snow fills the valley bottom, covering the green grass between limestone walls. The dale narrows ahead, ash trees leaning over the white carpet that muffles your footsteps.
The limestone pavement cracks under your boots, each fissure filled with white crystals. Ice has split the grey slabs into sharp-edged fragments.
The ash trees stand bare against the valley walls, their black branches brittle with frost. When you brush against them, ice falls like broken glass.
The stream runs silent beneath a skin of ice. Bubbles move slowly underneath, trapped between water and frozen surface.
Your breath steams white in the cave mouth. Water has frozen into clear curtains hanging from the limestone roof.
The valley floor crunches underfoot where frost has turned mud to iron. Each step leaves a dark print in the silver grass.
The grey limestone stretches flat beneath your feet, its cracks softened by the thin haze that hangs between the ash trees.
Water runs clear over white stone, the sound sharper in the muffled air.
The valley walls rise through layers of mist, their edges blurred until only the nearest outcrops hold their shape.
Ash leaves hang still in the grey light, droplets gathering on their edges where the haze thickens.
The cave mouth breathes cool air into the warm haze, stirring the mist that pools in the limestone hollows.
The sandstone glows amber in the direct sun. Purple heather stretches across the flat heath, each bloom sharp against the pale sky.
Your shadow falls black and crisp on the red-gold rock face. The overhang cuts a clean line of darkness above your head.
Birch leaves catch the light like hammered silver coins. The white bark stands bright against the warm stone behind.
Heat rises from the sandstone in wavering lines. The heather releases its honey scent into the still air.
Every ridge and crack in the rock face shows clear in the harsh light. The heath rolls away to the horizon without haze.
Rain drums against the sandstone overhang above your head. Water streams down the warm rock face in dark ribbons.
Purple heather bows under the weight of collected raindrops. Each step releases the sharp scent of bruised stems and wet earth.
The silver birch bark runs black with rain. Drops gather at the tips of hanging branches before falling to the heath below.
Water pools in the sandstone's carved hollows. The rock's orange and red streaks darken to rust and amber.
Rain hisses through the heather around your boots. The overhang provides shelter but not silence from the steady downpour.
Water beads on purple heather bells. The flowers hang heavy, darkened to bruise-black where the drizzle clings.
Your boots slip on wet sandstone. The rock face weeps thin streams where water finds each crack and seam.
Silver birch bark peels in damp ribbons. Drops gather at leaf tips, falling with soft ticks onto the heath below.
The overhang drips steadily. Water pools in the hollow beneath, turning the sandy floor to paste.
Mist clings to the warm stone. The sandstone's amber and rust fade to grey through the fine rain.
The fog swallows the heather ten paces ahead. Purple blooms float in grey nothing, cut from their stems.
Birch trunks emerge white from the mist like bone posts. Their silver bark weeps condensation down the grooves.
Your boots scrape against hidden sandstone. The warm orange rock appears sudden underfoot, vanishes again behind you.
An overhang looms from the fog wall. Water drips steady from its lip onto the stone below.
Heather brushes wet against your shins. The purple bells shed droplets that soak through wool.
The wind strips purple petals from the heather and hurls them against the sandstone. They stick to the warm rock face for a moment, then tear free.
Silver birch leaves flash their pale undersides as the branches whip back and forth. The trunks creak and bend but do not break.
Gusts funnel through the sandstone overhangs with a hollow whistle. The sound echoes off the curved walls and fades to nothing.
Your hair lashes across your face as you press against the sheltered side of an overhang. The rock feels warm against your back despite the cold wind.
Heather stems bow flat against the ground, their purple blooms crushed low. When the gust passes, they spring upright again.
Thunder splits the air above the sandstone overhang. Rain streams down the warm rock face in dark ribbons.
The heather bends flat against the stone, purple bells drumming with water. Wind strips silver leaves from the birch trees.
Lightning illuminates the red sandstone cliffs for one white moment. The thunder follows, echoing off the rock walls.
Water pools in the sandstone hollows, turning the dust to red mud. The birch bark peels in the driving rain.
Hail bounces off the stone ledge with sharp clicks. The heather releases its bitter scent into the wet air.
Snow catches in the heather's purple bells. The tiny crystals melt against the dark stems, leaving drops that glisten.
White flakes settle on the sandstone's warm ochre face. Steam rises where snow meets sun-heated rock.
The birch bark shows silver-white against falling snow. Black marks on the trunk stand sharp in the pale air.
Snow gathers in the rock's deep overhangs. The sheltered stone remains dry and rust-colored while flakes swirl outside.
Your boots crunch through snow into the springy heather beneath. Purple flowers show through the thin white layer.
Your breath steams white against the red sandstone overhang. Ice crystals coat the purple heather bells, turning them silver in the morning light.
The frozen ground rings hollow under your feet. Silver birch bark peels in frozen curls, crackling when you brush past.
Frost transforms the heather into a field of glass needles. The warm sandstone face radiates no heat through the bitter air.
Ice glazes the rock overhang in thick sheets. Your fingers stick briefly to the stone when you steady yourself against it.
The birch leaves hang black and brittle on white branches. Frozen heather stems snap underfoot with sharp cracks.
The haze softens the sandstone's edges. Purple heather bleeds into gray air.
Birch leaves shimmer through the thin veil. Their silver bark catches what light filters down.
The rock overhangs fade to suggestions in the mist. You duck under shapes you cannot quite see.
Heather bells hang heavy with moisture. The purple blooms darken to wine in the diffused light.
Warm sandstone radiates heat through the haze. Your palm finds the rough surface still sun-warmed.
Wind cuts across the granite tors without shelter. Your eyes water as you squint into the gusts that bend the tussock grass flat.
The bog squelches black water between hummocks of pale grass. Clear sky reflects in the pools like scattered mirrors.
Lichen crusts the granite in patches of yellow and grey. The rock face shows every crack and crystal in the sharp light.
Oak trees huddle twisted in the valley bottom. Their leaves rattle dry in the constant wind.
Your boots sink into sphagnum moss that springs back wet. The air smells of peat and the metallic tang of exposed stone.
Rain beads on the granite tor, running in dark streaks down its weathered face. The lichen grows slick and bright orange against the wet stone.
Your boots sink into the saturated peat between tussocks. Water seeps up around the leather with each step, cold against your ankles.
The stunted oak in the valley bottom drips steadily. Each leaf channels water into streams that patter against the bracken below.
Wind drives the rain horizontal across the moor. It stings your face and turns the bog pools into circles of jumping silver.
The tussock grass bends under the weight of collected rainwater. It releases cold showers against your legs as you push through.
Water beads on the granite's grey surface. Your boots slip on wet lichen that clings orange and yellow to the stone.
The tussock grass bends under droplets too fine to see. Each step releases the smell of peat and standing water.
Drizzle collects in the hollows of weathered granite. The tor disappears and reappears as mist shifts across the moor.
Stunted oaks in the valley drip steadily. Their leaves hang heavy, darkened from grey-green to black.
Bog cotton droops with moisture. Your footsteps squelch in ground that yields like wet sponge.
The fog swallows the granite tor twenty paces ahead. Only the wet crunch of tussock grass marks your steps.
Lichen-crusted stone emerges from white nothing as you reach forward. The surface runs slick with condensed mist.
Water squelches up from hidden bog with each footfall. The fog muffles the sound to a soft gulp.
A stunted oak materializes beside you, its twisted branches dripping. The trunk vanishes again above shoulder height.
Wind pushes the fog in gray waves across the moor. For moments you glimpse distant granite, then it's gone.
Wind shears across the granite tors, whistling through cracks in the stone. Yellow lichen flattens against the rock face.
Tussock grass bends low, stems whipping your shins as you walk. The bog water ripples in dark patches between the clumps.
Oak branches thrash in the valley below, leaves torn loose and spinning upward. The wind carries their dry rattle across the moor.
Your boots slip on wet granite as wind pushes against your back. Moss peels from the rock in thin green strips.
The bog squelches underfoot while wind drives cold spray from standing water. Sedge heads bow and snap back with each gust.
Lightning splits the sky above the granite tors. Thunder rolls across the moorland, echoing off stone.
Rain pounds the tussock grass into submission. Water pools in the bog, turning peat black as tar.
The stunted oak in the valley bends double against the gale. Leaves rip free and spiral upward.
Wind howls through gaps in the granite. Lichen streams from rock faces like wet hair.
Hail strikes the moor with sharp cracks. Ice bounces off stone and disappears into sodden grass.
Snow settles in the hollows between granite boulders. The tussock grass bends under white weight.
Flakes catch on yellow lichen crusting the tor's face. Wind drives snow horizontal across the open moor.
The stunted oak's bare branches collect snow in their angles. Bogwater shows black between white sedge clumps.
Snow fills your bootprints in the peat. Each step breaks through crust into dark water below.
White powder dusts the granite's rough surface. The wind scours snow from the tor's windward side.
The tussock grass cracks underfoot, each blade sheathed in white ice. Your breath steams in the still air.
Frost coats the granite tors in crystal scales. The wind has died, leaving only the snap of your footsteps on frozen bog.
Ice fills the spaces between lichen patches, turning the rock face into a patchwork of grey and silver. The granite radiates cold through your palms.
The stunted oaks stand black-branched in the valley, their bark split by frost. Water pools have turned to clouded mirrors beneath them.
Frozen peat yields like stone under your weight. The bog cotton stands rigid, each seed head rimmed with ice crystals.
The haze softens the granite tors until they blur like grey thumbs pressed into white cloth. Wind hisses through the tussock grass, each blade sharp against your ankles.
Lichen crusts the stone in patches of yellow and black. The soft light makes the bog cotton glow like scattered bones.
Your boots squelch in the dark peat between grass clumps. The haze swallows the distant tors, leaving only the nearest rocks visible as dark shapes.
Stunted oaks crouch in the valley fold, their leaves rustling dry in the constant wind. The thin light turns their bark silver-grey.
Water seeps cold through the bog moss under your feet. The haze drifts between the granite outcrops, muffling the wind's voice to a whisper.
Sunlight cuts sharp lines between the oak trunks. The valley walls rise black on both sides, their slate faces gleaming wet despite the clear sky.
Water rushes white over dark stones. The sound echoes off the valley sides, bouncing back and forth until it fills the narrow space completely.
Ferns catch the light in bright green patches. They carpet the valley floor between the trees, their fronds still heavy with yesterday's rain.
The river runs fast and shallow here. You can see every pebble on the bottom through the clear water, brown and grey and black.
Moss grows thick on the north-facing rocks. It glows almost yellow-green where the sun finds it through the canopy above.
Water streams down the slate faces in silver threads. The rock turns black under the rain.
Your boots slip on wet fern fronds. Oak leaves drip steadily onto the moss below.
The river runs brown with runoff from the hillsides. It churns around the dark stones.
Rain drums against the hazel canopy overhead. Droplets collect on spider webs stretched between branches.
The valley floor squelches underfoot. Water pools in every depression between the slate outcrops.
The drizzle beads on oak leaves above your head. Water drips from branch to branch, striking the ferns below with soft taps.
Slate chips glisten black under your boots. The river runs brown with runoff, foam catching against the dark stones.
Moss grows thick on the valley walls, bright green in the grey light. Your fingers come away wet when you brush against it.
The drizzle muffles the sound of rushing water below. Hazel branches bend low, heavy with collected moisture.
Water runs in thin streams down the slate faces. The air tastes of wet earth and rotting leaves.
The fog presses against the slate walls, muffling the rush of water below. You cannot see the river but hear it cutting through the valley floor.
Oak branches emerge from gray nothing, their bark slick with moisture. Moss hangs in thick curtains from every trunk.
Your boots slip on wet slate fragments scattered across the steep path. The fog swallows your footsteps three paces behind you.
Ferns brush against your legs, heavy with collected mist. Water drips steadily from their fronds onto the dark stone.
The valley walls vanish into white above you. Only the closest hazel stems show their scratched bark through the dense air.

The wind howls through the narrow valley, stripping yellow leaves from the oak branches above. They spiral down past wet slate faces, catching briefly on fern fronds before tumbling into the rushing water below.
Hazel branches whip and crack overhead. The river runs black between moss-slick stones, its voice lost beneath the wind's roar.
